prs.title: fix: unify scroll button and fix floating UI spacing issue
prs.body:

This PR unifies the scroll navigation system across the platform by replacing the previous dual-button setup (ScrollToTop + ScrollToBottom) with a single toggle scroll button (as implemented in the community page). It also fixes a floating UI spacing inconsistency between the scroll button and the Add Note icon on the Home and Visualizer pages.

## Related Issues
Closes #1753

## Changes Made
- Replaced separate scroll-to-top and scroll-to-bottom buttons with a single toggle scroll button across all pages
- Ensured consistent scroll behavior matching About/Team page implementation
- Removed duplicate floating scroll controls
- Fixed floating UI spacing issue between scroll button and Add Note icon on Home and Visualizer pages
- Prevented layout shift when scroll button toggles state

prs.title: fix: preserve resource page navigation when returning from visualizer
prs.body:
## Summary

This PR fixes the browser back-navigation flow when opening a resource from the Practice page and then navigating to the Visualizer via Explore Resource.

Previously, after opening a resource and clicking Explore Resource, pressing the browser Back button redirected users to the Problems page instead of returning them to the previously viewed resource page. This PR updates the navigation flow so browser history is preserved correctly and users are taken back to the same resource page they came from.

prs.title: Fix notification dropdown outside click behavior
prs.body:
## Summary

Fixes the notification dropdown behavior by automatically closing it when users click outside the dropdown area.

Previously, the dropdown remained open until the notification bell was clicked again, which was inconsistent with standard dropdown interactions and could obstruct the UI while navigating the application.

## Related Issues

Closes #1239 

## Changes Made

- Added a dedicated notificationRef for the notification dropdown.
- Implemented click-outside detection using useEffect and mousedown event listeners.
- Automatically closes the dropdown when clicking outside its container.
- Added proper cleanup of event listeners to prevent memory leaks.

issues.title: [BUG] Inconsistent card styling in Quiz Mode, Smart Revision, and Collaborative Sessions on Visualizer page
issues.body:
### Bug Description

The cards for Quiz Mode, Smart Revision, and Collaborative Sessions on the Visualizer page use a different visual theme compared to the rest of the algorithm cards.

While most algorithm cards (Array, Stack, Tree, Graph, etc.) follow a consistent dark theme and layout pattern, these three cards appear visually inconsistent (different styling/spacing/background treatment), which breaks UI uniformity and reduces overall design consistency.

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Go to 'AlgoBuddy Home / Visualizer page'
2. Scroll down to the algorithm cards section
3. Compare standard cards (Array, Stack, Tree, etc.)
4. Notice Quiz Mode, Smart Revision, and Collaborative Sessions cards
5. Observe inconsistent styling compared to other cards


### Expected Behavior

All cards on the Visualizer page should follow a consistent design system (same theme, spacing, border radius, typography, and hover effects), including Quiz Mode, Smart Revision, and Collaborative Sessions.

### Actual Behavior

Quiz Mode, Smart Revision, and Collaborative Sessions cards use a slightly different or unmatched theme compared to the rest of the algorithm cards, making them visually inconsistent within the grid.

### Environment

macOS / Windows (whichever applies), Chrome latest version AlgoBuddy Web App (Visualizer Page)

issues.title: Title: [BUG] Browser back button does not return to the previously selected problem section/tab on sheet problem pages
issues.body:
### Bug Description

On sheet/problem pages, navigation between sections such as Problems and Description is not being handled properly in browser history.

When a user opens a sheet page, clicks on a problem, and then navigates to another section like Description, pressing the browser Back button does not return the user to the previously selected section/state as expected. Instead, it takes the user somewhere else / out of the expected page flow.

This makes in-page navigation feel broken because section changes are not being reflected correctly in browser history.

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Open a sheet/problem page (for example a roadmap sheet page that has tabs like Problems and Description).
2. Click on a problem from the Problems section.
3. Navigate to the Description section/tab.
4. Press the browser Back button.
5. Observe the navigation behavior.


### Expected Behavior

Pressing the browser Back button after moving between sections like Problems and Description should return the user to the previous section / state within the same page flow.

For example:

- open a sheet/problem page
- go to Problems
- click a problem / move to Description
- press Back
- it should return to the problem section / previous in-page state, instead of navigating somewhere unrelated

The back button should preserve the user’s navigation context within the sheet/problem page.

### Actual Behavior

Instead of returning to the previously selected problem section/state, the browser Back button navigates somewhere else, which breaks the expected page flow and makes navigation confusing.

### Environment

macOS Chrome

### Code of Conduct

- [x] I agree to follow this project's Code of Conduct

issues.title: [BUG] Browser back button does not preserve topic selection history on Topic-wise Practice page

2. Click on one topic, for example Array.

5. Observe the navigation behavior.

### Expected Behavior

Example:

- click Linked List

This would make topic selection behave like a proper navigable UI state.

After switching between topics, pressing the browser Back button skips the previous topic state and takes the user directly back to the problem list / previous page instead of restoring the previously selected topic.

issues.title: Title: Unify floating scroll navigation button behavior across pages
issues.body:
## Description

On the **About / Team page** (the page with founder profile, team members, contributors, footer, etc.), there is a **single floating scroll button** that behaves intelligently:

* when the user is **not at the bottom**, it works as **Scroll to Bottom**
* when the user **reaches the bottom**, it changes to **Scroll to Top**

This behavior feels much cleaner and more functional compared to other pages, where there are currently **two separate floating buttons** for:

* scroll to top
* scroll to bottom

## Problem

The current scroll navigation UI is inconsistent across the platform:

* **About / Team page** → uses **one dynamic toggle button**
* **other pages** → use **two separate buttons**

Because of this, the experience feels inconsistent and visually a bit cluttered on the pages that still use two buttons.

## Expected behavior

The same **single dynamic floating scroll button** used on the About / Team page should be implemented **everywhere across AlgoBuddy** where top/bottom scroll navigation is needed.

### Desired behavior of the unified button

* If the user is **not near the bottom of the page**, the button should act as **Scroll to Bottom**
* If the user is **at / near the bottom of the page**, the button should switch to **Scroll to Top**

## Why this change would help

* makes the UI more consistent across all pages
* reduces visual clutter by replacing two buttons with one
* improves usability with a cleaner scroll navigation pattern
* reuses an already existing behavior from the About / Team page instead of having multiple patterns

## Suggested scope

Apply this single toggle scroll button pattern on pages that currently show separate top/bottom floating scroll controls, so the navigation behavior remains consistent throughout the platform.

issues.title: [UI/UX] Algorithm Notebook Editor Font Size Is Too Small and Difficult to Read
issues.body:
## Description

The text editor used in the Algorithm Notebook has a very small default font size, making notes difficult to read and write comfortably. Since the Algorithm Notebook is accessible throughout the platform, this readability issue affects users across multiple pages and workflows.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Open the Algorithm Notebook from any page where it is available.
2. Create a new note or open an existing note.
3. Observe the font size used within the note editor.

## Expected Behavior

The notebook editor should use a readable font size that allows users to comfortably read and write notes without excessive zooming or eye strain.

## Actual Behavior

The editor text appears too small, making note-taking and reviewing notes less comfortable, especially during extended usage sessions.

## Environment

* OS: macOS
* Browser: Chrome
* Version: Latest available version

## Impact

* Reduced readability across the platform.
* Poor accessibility for users with visual impairments.
* Less comfortable note-taking experience during long study sessions.

## Suggested Fix

* Increase the default font size of the Algorithm Notebook editor.
* Consider providing font-size customization options (e.g., Small, Medium, Large) to improve accessibility and user preference support.

issues.title: [FEATURE] Add Back to Top button on Cheatsheets, Roadmaps, Blog & FAQ pages
issues.body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

Users have to manually scroll back to the top on long pages such as Cheatsheets, Roadmaps, Blog, and FAQ. This becomes inconvenient and reduces overall navigation experience, especially on content-heavy pages.

### Describe the solution you'd like

I would like a reusable Back to Top button component to be added across multiple pages.

The button should:
- Appear only when the user scrolls down a certain distance
- Smoothly scroll the page back to the top when clicked
- Be consistent with the existing UI design system
- Be reusable so it can be added to multiple pages without duplicating code

Pages to include:
- Cheatsheets
- Roadmaps
- Blog
- FAQ

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Users currently rely on manual scrolling or browser scroll-to-top functionality. However, this is not user-friendly for long pages and requires unnecessary effort. A dedicated UI button provides a much smoother and modern navigation experience.

### Additional context

This feature improves UX consistency across all long-form content pages in AlgoBuddy. It aligns with modern web design practices and enhances usability for users navigating large amounts of content.

### Code of Conduct

issues.title: Feature: Add GitHub Repository Icon in Footer for Easy Access to Source Code
issues.body:
###  Problem Statement

Currently, the Travel-Plans website does not provide a direct link to the project's GitHub repository. Users and potential contributors visiting the website have no convenient way to access the source code, report issues, or contribute to the project.

This can reduce project visibility and make it harder for developers interested in contributing to find the repository.

###  Proposed Solution

Add a GitHub icon to the website footer that redirects users to the official GitHub repository.

Suggested implementation:

- Place a GitHub icon alongside existing footer links or branding.
- Redirect users to the project's GitHub repository when clicked.
- Open the repository in a new tab using: (target="_blank"rel="noopener noreferrer")
- Apply hover effects consistent with the existing UI design.

###  Alternatives Considered

- Adding a "View Source" text link instead of an icon.
- Including the repository link in the navigation bar.
- Creating a dedicated "Contribute" section containing the repository link.

However, a GitHub icon in the footer is a clean, widely recognized, and space-efficient solution.

###  Additional Context

Adding a GitHub repository link is a common practice in open-source projects and improves transparency, accessibility, and contributor engagement.

issues.title: [FEATURE]: Improve Bookmarked Problems Functionality
issues.body:
### Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

The Bookmarked Problems section on the Dashboard displays bookmarked questions correctly, but it lacks essential functionality for managing and navigating bookmarks.

Currently:

- Users cannot remove a problem directly from the Bookmarked Problems section.
- The topic badge/category displayed on each bookmarked problem (e.g., Tree, Linked List, Graph) is not clickable and does not redirect users to the corresponding module on the Practice page.

This creates an unnecessary navigation burden and makes bookmark management less convenient.

### Describe the solution you'd like

Add the following enhancements to the Bookmarked Problems section:

1. Unbookmark Functionality

- Add an Unbookmark/Remove Bookmark button or icon on each bookmarked problem card.
- Allow users to remove bookmarks directly from the Dashboard.
- Update the bookmarked list instantly after removal.

2. Topic-Based Navigation

- Make the topic/category badge clickable.
- Clicking a topic should redirect users to the corresponding module on the Practice page.

These improvements will make bookmarked problems easier to manage and help users quickly continue practicing related questions within the same topic.

### Describe alternatives you've considered

Currently, users must navigate to the original problem page to manage bookmarks, which requires additional clicks and interrupts workflow.

For topic navigation, users have to manually open the Practice page and locate the relevant module themselves. Making topic badges clickable would provide a much more intuitive experience.

### Additional context

Example scenario:

A user bookmarks "Binary Tree Inorder Traversal" under the Tree topic.

Expected behavior:

- The user can directly remove the bookmark from the Dashboard.
- Clicking the "Tree" topic badge opens the Tree module on the Practice page, allowing the user to continue solving related problems.

This enhancement improves both usability and navigation within the learning workflow.

issues.title: [FEATURE]: Add "Back to Top" Button on Practice Page
issues.body:
## Summary

Add a floating "Back to Top" button on the Practice page that allows users to quickly return to the top of the page with a single click.

## Problem it Solves

The Practice page contains multiple DSA topic sections and cards, making it a long page to navigate. Users who scroll down to explore topics currently have to manually scroll all the way back to the top to access the search bar or revisit the beginning of the page. - Adding a browser-dependent scroll-to-top behavior.

However, a dedicated button provides a more intuitive and accessible user experience across devices.

## Additional Context

This enhancement would improve navigation and overall usability of the Practice page, particularly for users browsing multiple DSA categories before returning to the top section.

issues.title: [FEATURE] : Add Kadane Algorithm Visualizer with Interactive Dashboard Card
issues.body:
## Summary

Add a new Kadane Algorithm visualizer to AlgoBuddy along with a dedicated card on the Visualizer dashboard for easy access and navigation.

## Problem it Solves

Currently, AlgoBuddy includes visualizers for multiple data structures and algorithms such as Queue, Linked List, Tree, and Graph, but it does not include a visualization for Kadane’s Algorithm (Maximum Subarray Sum).

Kadane’s Algorithm is a commonly used dynamic programming algorithm and would be a valuable addition for users learning array optimization techniques.

## Proposed Solution

Implement a complete Kadane Algorithm visualizer with:

- Step-by-step visualization of current sum and maximum sum
- Highlighting of active elements/subarrays
- Interactive input for custom arrays
- Animation controls (play/reset/speed)
- Code implementation section similar to existing visualizers
- Responsive UI matching the current AlgoBuddy design system

Additionally, add a dedicated “Kadane Algorithm” card on the main Visualizer page similar to the existing Queue, Linked List, Tree, and Graph cards.

## Benefits

- Expands AlgoBuddy’s algorithm coverage
- Helps users understand dynamic programming concepts visually
- Improves learning experience through interactive visualization
- Maintains consistency with the existing visualizer dashboard

issues.title: [FEATURE]: Add client-side email validation with inline error feedback in authentication form
issues.body:
## Summary

The authentication form currently allows invalid email inputs (e.g., abc, user123, test) without showing any validation message or preventing submission. This leads to poor user experience and unnecessary backend requests.

## Problem it Solves

Users can enter invalid email formats without any immediate feedback. The system only validates this after submission or fails silently, which can confuse users and degrade UX.

## Proposed Solution

Add client-side validation for email input before form submission.

Possible improvements:

- Validate email format using HTML (type="email")
- Show inline error message below input field (e.g., “Please enter a valid email address”)
- Prevent form submission until input is valid
- Optional: disable submit button when invalid input is present

## Alternatives Considered

- Relying only on backend validation (not ideal for UX)
- Using only HTML5 validation without custom error messages

## Additional Context

This improvement will enhance user experience, reduce invalid authentication requests, and align the form with standard authentication UX practices.
It also improves perceived responsiveness of the login system.

issues.title: [Feature]: Improve Light Mode UI Theme for Better Visual Appeal
issues.body:
### Problem or limitation

The current light mode is not fully optimized for extended use, as it lacks a cohesive and modern visual design system. Compared to the dark mode, the light theme appears less refined, with inconsistencies in visual hierarchy, contrast, and component styling. As a result, the overall user experience in light mode feels less visually balanced, particularly for users operating in well-lit environments or those who prefer light interfaces.

### Proposed solution

AlgoScope should introduce an improved light mode design system with a consistent and modern color palette, better contrast, and improved visual hierarchy.

Key improvements:

- Define a proper light theme system (background, surface, primary, secondary colors)
- Improve contrast for readability across all components
- Add subtle shadows/elevation to cards, panels, and modals
- Enhance button styles and hover states for better interactivity feedback
- Ensure consistency across all modules (visualizers, controls, sidebar, etc.)
- Optionally align light mode styling closer to the design quality of dark mode

### Alternatives considered

Another possible approach would be to keep the current light mode unchanged and only make minor tweaks (like adjusting contrast or button colors). However, this would not fully address the overall lack of cohesion and modern UI feel. A more complete theme improvement would provide a better long-term solution.

### Use case

This would help when users are using AlgoScope in bright environments (daytime study sessions, classrooms, or presentations) where light mode is preferred.

It would also improve accessibility and readability for users who do not prefer dark themes, and enhance the overall first impression of the application for new users exploring the platform.

### Additional context

AlgoScope is a highly visual learning platform for DSA, and UI quality directly impacts user engagement and learning experience. Since animations and visualizations are already strong, improving the light mode UI would make the overall product feel more complete and production-ready.

Reference idea: modern design systems like Material UI, shadcn/ui-inspired light themes, or Notion-style soft light interfaces could be considered for inspiration.

issues.title: [Bug]: Back navigation skips previous page in Binary Tree visualizer flow
issues.body:
In the Algorithm Visualizer, the back navigation inside the Binary Tree section is not working as expected.

After opening any Binary Tree algorithm detail page, clicking the back arrow does not return to the immediate previous page. Instead, it skips one intermediate page and navigates two steps back in the browser history.

This breaks the expected step-by-step navigation flow while exploring Binary Tree algorithms.


Steps to Reproduce

1. Open Algorithm Visualizer
2. Navigate to Tree → Binary Tree
3. Click on any Binary Tree option
4. Open its detail page
5. Click the back arrow (←)
6. Observe incorrect navigation behavior


Expected Behavior

Back button should navigate to the immediate previous page in the Binary Tree flow.


Actual Behavior

Back button skips one page and navigates two steps back in history.

issues.title: [Feature]: Allow Custom Array Input for Sorting Visualizers
issues.body:
### Problem or limitation

Currently, the sorting visualizers only use randomly generated arrays. This limits the ability to test and observe algorithm behavior on specific input cases such as sorted arrays, reverse-sorted arrays, or user-defined datasets. As a result, the learning and experimentation experience is somewhat restricted.

### Proposed solution

AlgoScope should support a custom array input feature that allows users to enter comma-separated numeric values (e.g., 5, 2, 8, 1, 9).

The application should:

- Parse and validate the user input
- Convert it into an array for visualization
- Replace the randomly generated array with the user-provided array when valid input is given
- Fall back to a default/random array in case of invalid or empty input

This will make the visualizer more interactive and flexible.

### Alternatives considered

An alternative approach would be to retain only random array generation but provide controls for adjusting array size and value range. However, this approach does not allow testing of specific custom cases, which is important for deeper understanding and experimentation with sorting algorithms.

### Use case

This feature would be useful for:

- Testing specific scenarios such as best-case, worst-case, or nearly sorted inputs
- Understanding how different input patterns affect sorting performance and steps
- Allowing users to experiment with their own datasets for better conceptual clarity
- Enhancing the overall interactivity and educational value of the visualizer


### Additional context

Adding support for custom input would significantly improve the flexibility and educational effectiveness of AlgoScope, making it more suitable for students and learners exploring sorting algorithms in depth.

issues.title: Fix layout crash caused by script tag in app/layout.jsx
issues.body:
## What’s happening
The app crashes on load because of a `<script>` tag inside `app/layout.jsx`.

Next.js throws an error since script tags inside React components are not allowed and it breaks rendering.

## Location
`app/layout.jsx` (around line 72)

## Error
Encountered a script tag while rendering React component. Scripts inside React components are never executed when rendering on the client.

## Expected behavior
The app should load normally without crashing on startup.
